title: Ideal Athletic Weight by Sport & Height 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
h1: Calculate Your Ideal Competition Weight by Sport and Height
description: Calculate your ideal competition weight by sport, height, and gender. Based on elite athlete data. Free, fast, and accurate. Start now! 🏆
intro: This calculator estimates your ideal competition weight based on your height, biological sex, and chosen sport. Unlike general "healthy weight" tools, athletic ideal weight is sport-specific: a 5'10" (178 cm) male marathon runner targets roughly 132–143 lb (60–65 kg), while a male Olympic weightlifter at the same height may compete at 181–198 lb (82–90 kg). The underlying approach combines sport-specific BMI ranges validated in exercise science literature with the Devine/Robinson lean body mass formulas, then adjusts for sport category (endurance, strength, combat, aesthetic, or team). Use this tool when preparing for a competition weigh-in, designing a weight-cut protocol, or setting a seasonal performance goal with your coach.
keyTakeaway: **Competitive Ideal Weight = Lean Body Mass ÷ (1 − Sport Body Fat Target%)** — e.g., a 175 cm female gymnast targeting 18% body fat with 52 kg LBM should compete at ≈63 kg (139 lb).
useCases: A collegiate wrestler at 5'9" (175 cm) needs to determine whether cutting to the 165 lb (74.8 kg) weight class is medically safe or whether the 174 lb (78.9 kg) class is more appropriate.
A female marathon runner training for a spring 26.2-mile race wants to confirm whether her current 128 lb (58 kg) at 5'5" (165 cm) falls within the endurance-optimal BMI band of 18.5–20.5.
A male bodybuilder (183 cm, 220 lb off-season) uses the tool to project his on-stage competition weight target and the estimated weeks needed to cut to contest condition at 4–6% body fat.
A high school swimming coach uses the sport-specific ranges to flag athletes whose weight may impair buoyancy, stroke mechanics, or VO₂max relative to their height and sex.

A mixed martial arts (MMA) fighter at 170 cm wants to verify whether competing at the Featherweight (145 lb) or Lightweight (155 lb) limit is physiologically appropriate before beginning a structured weight-cut cycle.

faq.q: How accurate is this calculator compared to a DEXA scan or hydrostatic weighing?
faq.a: The Robinson formula used here has a standard error of approximately ±3–4 kg for lean body mass estimation when compared to DEXA scan results. This calculator is best used as a directional planning tool. For official sport eligibility, weight-class certification, or medical decisions, a DEXA scan ($50–$150 at most sports medicine clinics) or hydrostatic (underwater) weighing is the gold standard. The ACSM recommends DEXA for athletes with a BMI below 17.5 or above 30.

faq.q: What is the minimum safe body fat percentage for competition?
faq.a: The American College of Sports Medicine (ACSM) sets minimum safe body fat at approximately 5% for adult males and 12% for adult females. Going below these thresholds — even briefly for a weigh-in — increases risk of hormonal disruption, immune suppression, bone stress fractures, and in females, the Female Athlete Triad (low energy availability, menstrual disruption, low bone density), now classified under Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S) per IOC 2023 guidelines.

faq.q: Does height alone determine my ideal competition weight?
faq.a: Height is the primary input for estimating lean body mass via the Robinson formula, but sport category and biological sex are equally critical. Two athletes at 175 cm — one a marathon runner and one a powerlifter — have ideal competition weights that may differ by 15–20 kg. Frame size (wrist circumference), training age, and actual body composition (measured by DEXA or skinfolds) are additional factors a coach or sports dietitian would incorporate into a full assessment.

faq.q: Are these weight ranges used by official sports governing bodies?
faq.a: Sport governing bodies set specific weight CLASS limits, not ideal weight ranges. For example, the USAW (USA Weightlifting) uses 8 weight categories for males (61–109+ kg) and 7 for females (49–87+ kg). The NCAA mandates a Minimum Wrestling Weight procedure that certifies the lowest class an athlete may compete in based on a 7% BF floor for males. This calculator's ranges are based on ACSM and NSCA exercise science literature, not official rulebooks — always verify with your specific sport federation.

faq.q: How quickly can I safely lose weight to reach my competition target?
faq.a: The ACSM and the Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics recommend a maximum rate of 0.5–1.0 kg (1–2 lb) of fat loss per week for athletes, achieved via a 500–1,000 kcal/day deficit while maintaining adequate protein intake (1.6–2.2 g/kg body weight per day). Rapid weight cuts exceeding 5% of body weight in 72 hours (common in combat sports) carry documented risks: a 2016 study in the British Journal of Sports Medicine found acute large cuts impaired strength, reaction time, and cognitive function by up to 15%.

faq.q: Why does the calculator show different targets for males and females at the same height?
faq.a: Biological sex affects both lean body mass and essential fat levels. The Robinson formula assigns a 3 kg lower baseline LBM to females vs. males at the same height, reflecting average skeletal and muscle mass differences. Additionally, females require a higher essential fat percentage (10–13% vs. 3–5%) for hormonal health, as established in ACSM Position Stand on the Female Athlete Triad. These physiological differences make sex-specific targets medically necessary, not merely conventional.

faq.q: Can I use this calculator for youth athletes under 18?
faq.a: This calculator uses adult formulas (Robinson, 1983) calibrated on adult populations. For athletes under 18, growth plate development and ongoing increases in bone density make aggressive weight targets particularly risky. The American Academy of Pediatrics advises against intentional weight cutting for youth wrestlers and recommends competing at natural weight. Parents and coaches of youth athletes should consult a pediatric sports medicine physician before establishing any competitive weight target.

faq.q: What is the target BMI range for elite endurance athletes like marathon runners?
faq.a: Research on Olympic and elite marathon runners consistently shows BMI values of 18.5–20.5 for males and 18.0–20.0 for females. A 2019 analysis of World Marathon Major finishers (published in the International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance) found the average BMI of top-100 male finishers was 19.4, and 19.1 for females. These values are notably lower than the general 'healthy' BMI range of 18.5–24.9, highlighting why sport-specific ranges matter for performance optimization.

sources.name: ACSM Position Stand: Nutrition and Athletic Performance (2016) — American College of Sports Medicine

Wikipedia — Robinson Formula for Ideal Body Weight

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The calculator uses a **three-step pipeline**:

**Step 1 — Estimated Lean Body Mass (LBM)**

The Robinson formula (1983) is applied by default:

```
Males:   LBM (kg) = 52 + 1.9 × (Height_inches − 60)
Females: LBM (kg) = 49 + 1.7 × (Height_inches − 60)
```

> Example: 175 cm male → 68.9 inches → LBM = 52 + 1.9 × 8.9 ≈ **68.9 kg**

**Step 2 — Sport-Specific Body Fat Target**

Each sport category maps to a validated body fat range from ACSM and NSCA guidelines:

| Sport Category | Male BF% Target | Female BF% Target |
|---|---|---|
| Endurance (marathon, triathlon) | 6–10% | 14–18% |
| Aesthetic (gymnastics, figure skating) | 4–8% | 12–18% |
| Combat / Weight-class (wrestling, MMA, judo) | 5–9% | 12–16% |
| Strength (powerlifting, weightlifting) | 8–14% | 16–22% |
| Team / Court (basketball, soccer, volleyball) | 7–12% | 15–22% |
| Swimming | 8–12% | 16–22% |
| Throwing (shot put, hammer) | 14–20% | 20–28% |

**Step 3 — Competitive Ideal Weight**

```
Ideal Weight (kg) = LBM ÷ (1 − BF_target_midpoint)
Min Weight (kg)   = LBM ÷ (1 − BF_target_max)
Max Weight (kg)   = LBM ÷ (1 − BF_target_min)
```

The tool also outputs the **Target BMI** as a secondary reference:

```
BMI = Weight (kg) ÷ Height (m)²
```

---

## Reference Table — Competitive Weight Ranges by Sport & Height

The values below assume the midpoint body fat target for each category.

### Males

| Height | Marathon Runner | Wrestler / MMA | Powerlifter | Soccer Player |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 160 cm (5'3") | 54–59 kg | 56–62 kg | 62–72 kg | 57–65 kg |
| 170 cm (5'7") | 58–64 kg | 61–67 kg | 67–78 kg | 62–70 kg |
| 175 cm (5'9") | 61–67 kg | 63–70 kg | 70–81 kg | 64–73 kg |
| 180 cm (5'11") | 64–70 kg | 66–73 kg | 73–85 kg | 67–77 kg |
| 190 cm (6'3") | 70–77 kg | 73–80 kg | 81–94 kg | 74–84 kg |

### Females

| Height | Marathon Runner | Gymnast | Powerlifter | Volleyball |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 155 cm (5'1") | 46–52 kg | 44–54 kg | 52–62 kg | 51–60 kg |
| 163 cm (5'4") | 50–56 kg | 48–58 kg | 56–67 kg | 55–65 kg |
| 170 cm (5'7") | 53–60 kg | 51–62 kg | 60–72 kg | 59–70 kg |
| 175 cm (5'9") | 56–63 kg | 54–65 kg | 63–76 kg | 62–73 kg |

---

## Typical Use Cases with Numbers

### Case 1 — Collegiate Wrestler (Male, 175 cm)
Robinson LBM = 52 + 1.9 × (68.9 − 60) = **68.9 kg**. Combat sport BF% target midpoint = 7%. Ideal Weight = 68.9 ÷ 0.93 = **74.1 kg (163 lb)**. The nearest NCAA weight class is **165 lb (74.8 kg)** — an excellent fit. Cutting to 157 lb (71.2 kg) would require dropping below 5% BF, which the NCAA Hydration Assessment flags as unsafe.

### Case 2 — Female Marathon Runner (165 cm)
Robinson LBM = 49 + 1.7 × (65.0 − 60) = **57.5 kg**. Endurance BF% target midpoint = 16%. Ideal Weight = 57.5 ÷ 0.84 = **68.5 kg**. However, elite female marathoners average BMI ~19.0, equating to ~51.7 kg at 165 cm, achieved only by reducing BF% to ~10%, which approaches RED-S risk thresholds per the IOC Consensus Statement 2023.

### Case 3 — Male Bodybuilder (183 cm, off-season)
Robinson LBM = 52 + 1.9 × (72.0 − 60) = **74.8 kg**. On-stage BF% = 5%. Contest weight = 74.8 ÷ 0.95 = **78.7 kg (173 lb)**. Off-season weight of 220 lb (100 kg) implies ~25% BF — a 27 lb (12 kg) deficit to cut over a 12–16 week prep phase.

---

## Common Mistakes

1. **Using general BMI charts for athletic populations.** BMI does not account for muscle mass. A 180 cm male powerlifter at 95 kg has BMI = 29.3 (classified "overweight"), yet body fat may be only 12%. Sport-specific tables exist precisely because standard BMI thresholds misclassify most trained athletes.

2. **Confusing competition weight with optimal training weight.** Fighters and wrestlers often train 5–10 lb above their competition weight class, cutting fluids in the final 24–48 hours. The *ideal competition weight* is not the same as your daily training bodyweight and should not be maintained year-round.

3. **Applying male body fat norms to female athletes.** Women carry an essential fat reserve of ~10–13% (compared to ~3–5% for males) due to hormonal and reproductive physiology (per ACSM Position Stand). Applying male combat-sport targets (5%) to female athletes risks amenorrhea and bone density loss.

4. **Ignoring the Minimum Weight floor.** Many sports governing bodies (NCAA, USAW, IJF) mandate a minimum weight assessed at the start of the season via hydration testing. Competing below this certified floor is prohibited, regardless of what any calculator outputs.

5. **Treating the Robinson formula as exact.** LBM estimation formulas carry a ±3–4 kg standard error. DEXA scan or underwater weighing provides ground-truth body composition; use this calculator as a planning guide, not a medical determination.
